_comment: SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for the study planner. Read by the P620 scripts (scripts/lib.sh → render-context.js at public/curriculum.json) AND by the web app (fetched at /curriculum.json). Track structure + skills live here; live progress (per-track month, skill coverage status) lives in config/status.json and the D1 skill_coverage table. Edit months/skills here, edit current positions in config/status.json.

roles.name: D.E. Shaw — Applied AI Engineer
roles.note: Production AI systems + ML engineering rigor.

roles.name: D.E. Shaw — Alternative Data Analyst
roles.note: Alt-data sourcing, signal research, statistical rigor.

roles.name: Aaru — Software Engineer, Data Integration
roles.note: Data pipelines, entity resolution, large-scale ingestion, alt-data. Interview Wed Jun 24.

roles.name: Equi — Founding Engineer, Applied AI Lead
roles.note: Full-stack AI (RAG, LLM workflows), TypeScript/Next.js, investment domain. Interview Tue Jun 23.

interviews.about: Predictive intelligence platform — AI agents simulate entire human populations at scale for geopolitical strategy, product innovation, and marketing. Data Integration is the foundation that powers all simulations.
your_strengths: M Science / Jefferies: processed large-scale alt-data for quant equity signals — entity resolution across fragmented vendor identifiers was core to the job
Bridges AI CTO: built production data pipelines end-to-end with quality monitoring, docs, and ownership
MS Data Science (GPA 3.84): statistical rigor — sampling biases, imputation, anomaly detection, validation logic
Strong Python: transformation code, custom parsers, schema harmonization scripts all in your wheelhouse

items: Blocking strategies: sorted neighborhood, LSH (MinHash / SimHash), canopy clustering — why blocking is necessary before pairwise comparison
Similarity metrics: Jaro-Winkler, token-sort ratio, phonetic (Soundex/Metaphone), embedding cosine similarity
ML-based ER: Splink (probabilistic, Fellegi-Sunter) vs. Dedupe (active-learning); know when to use each
Scalable ER at 100M+ record scale: push blocking to Snowflake/Spark, pull candidate pairs for scoring
Handling fragmented / noisy identifiers: composite keys, fuzzy join, cross-walk tables
Medallion architecture: Bronze (raw) → Silver (cleaned/deduped) → Gold (aggregated/linked) — articulate trade-offs
Incremental vs. full-refresh: CDC (change data capture), watermarks, append-only vs. SCD Type 2 for history
Schema evolution: backward-compatible changes, additive-only column additions, Avro / Iceberg schema registry patterns
Idempotent + restartable design: write-audit-publish, dead-letter queues, exactly-once semantics
Partitioning strategy: date partitions for time-series, hash partitions for entity-keyed data
Window functions: LAG/LEAD, RANK/DENSE_RANK, NTILE, running totals with ROWS BETWEEN
CTEs and recursive CTEs for hierarchical / graph data (org charts, entity hierarchies)
Lateral joins and UNNEST for nested / array columns (Snowflake VARIANT, BigQuery ARRAY)
Query optimization: partition pruning, clustering keys in Snowflake, materialized views, EXPLAIN plan reading
Slowly Changing Dimensions: Type 1 (overwrite), Type 2 (versioned rows), Type 4 (history table)
Core DQ dimensions: completeness, uniqueness, timeliness, consistency, referential integrity
Tooling: Great Expectations / Soda / Pandera — how to write a data contract and gate a pipeline
Statistical profiling: null rates, value distribution shifts, cardinality drift, Z-score anomaly detection
Alerting: p95 row-count thresholds, freshness SLAs, PagerDuty / Slack webhook integration
Documentation: data dictionaries, lineage graphs (dbt docs, OpenLineage), column-level descriptions
Snowflake: micro-partitions, clustering keys, zero-copy cloning, Streams + Tasks for CDC, COPY INTO
BigQuery: partitioned/clustered tables, federated queries, INFORMATION_SCHEMA for metadata, slot reservations
Databricks: Delta Lake (ACID, time-travel), Unity Catalog, Auto Loader for streaming ingestion
Orchestration: Airflow (DAGs, sensors, XComs) vs. Dagster (assets, software-defined partitions) — know the difference
dbt: models (staging/intermediate/marts), tests (not_null, unique, relationships), snapshots for SCD2, macros
Alt-data types: transaction data, clickstream, geospatial / point-of-interest, satellite imagery, NLP (earnings calls, news)
Sourcing and linkage: CUSIP/ISIN/ticker cross-walks, address normalization, phone/email hashing for identity resolution
Data licensing: DUAs, redistribution restrictions, derived-data rules — when you can/can't re-share signals
PII handling: tokenization, SHA-256 hashing, k-anonymity, pseudonymization vs. anonymization
Bias awareness: survivorship bias in vendor panels, look-ahead bias in point-in-time data, bot-traffic contamination in clickstream

story_beats: M Science / Jefferies: 'At M Science, I ingested and cross-linked multiple alt-data vendor feeds to construct quant signals. Entity resolution across fragmented company identifiers — tickers, CIK, CUSIP — was a core daily challenge at scale.'
Bridges AI: 'As CTO I owned the full data stack — ingestion, cleaning, validation, and docs. I built the pipeline with data quality gates so downstream AI features had reliable inputs.'

MS Data Science: 'My stats background shapes how I think about data issues — I treat every new dataset with skepticism: what are the sampling biases, what's the missingness pattern, what would break if this feed went stale?'
likely_questions: Walk me through a pipeline you built that handled messy, large-scale data.
How do you approach entity resolution across two datasets with noisy identifiers?
What does a good data quality framework look like to you?
How do you decide between full-refresh and incremental ingestion?
Tell me about a time a data issue caused a downstream problem — how did you catch and fix it?
What alt-data sources have you worked with and how did you validate them?
How do you think about privacy and compliance when integrating third-party data?
How have you handled schema changes without breaking downstream consumers?
What's your mental model for debugging a pipeline that's silently producing wrong data?

rounds.title: Recruiter / Hiring Screen
rounds.format: Lead with M Science alt-data (satellite, job postings, POS) -> Bridges AI Valorem pipeline. Have 60s answers cold.

rounds.title: Technical Take-Home or Live Data Exercise
rounds.format: Multi-source CSV/JSON with overlapping, inconsistent entities: clean, join, dedupe, flag quality. Reuse the 9-section skeleton.

rounds.title: System Design / Architecture
rounds.format: Design a pipeline to ingest a new 3rd-party source and integrate it with existing population data at >100TB. Medallion, idempotency, lineage.

rounds.title: Behavioral
rounds.format: Filters for urgency, intellectual honesty, ownership, frontier curiosity. STAR but compressed to ~90s.

questions_to_ask.q: How does the data integration team's work plug into the simulation/agent layer day to day - once-and-done pipelines per partner, or a standardized ingestion framework over time?
When you onboard a brand-new third-party data source, what does your validation process look like before it's trusted enough to feed a simulation?
With the Series A and partners like Accenture and EY depending on simulation accuracy, how has the bar for data quality/governance changed as you've scaled?
What's the messiest data source you've had to integrate so far, and what made it hard?

behavioral_bank.prompt: Integrate messy, untrustworthy data under time pressure.
behavioral_bank.story: Woodline take-home (mixed date formats) or M Science Industrials alt-data - pick the one you can speak to with the most technical depth live.

behavioral_bank.prompt: Own something end-to-end with minimal oversight.
behavioral_bank.story: Bridges AI Valorem build - 13-service architecture, you as CTO, no one to defer to.

behavioral_bank.prompt: A decision made with incomplete information.
behavioral_bank.story: A Valorem architecture call or the home-lab LiteLLM routing decision - frame as calculated, reversible, fast.
A real Bridges AI co-founder disagreement or a CU Boulder group project - concrete, resolution-focused.

behavioral_bank.prompt: Why data integration, why Aaru, why now?
behavioral_bank.story: M Science (alt data as a research lens) -> Bridges AI (you build the pipelines) -> genuine interest in agent-based population simulation as the next frontier.

final_checklist: Re-read the Part 3 take-home framework the morning of any take-home
Practice the entity-resolution notebook end-to-end, timed
Rehearse the 5 behavioral stories out loud, 90s each
Have 3 questions ready, tailored to who you're meeting
Review Aaru's EY and Accenture case studies for fresh, specific language
Know your M Science and Valorem data-quality stories cold

practice.title: Entity-resolution take-home
practice.desc: Timed 9-section skeleton: inspect -> quality log -> entity resolution -> schema harmonization -> join -> validation -> summary, over panel + survey data.

practice.title: Reference solution
practice.desc: Open only after attempting it cold under a timer.

practice.title: Synthetic data + generator
practice.desc: panel_data.csv + survey_data.csv (same population, two sources) and generate_data.py.

interviews.about: Investment management firm — institutional-quality alternative investments (fund of funds). Builds white-label fund of funds products for multi-family offices and advisory firms. AUM scaling rapidly. You'd be the first/lead engineer building AI systems for manager selection, portfolio construction, and investment workflow automation.
your_strengths: RAG systems and LLM workflows (strong): directly maps to their AI engineering needs — parsing fund docs, building investment intelligence
Agentic systems: investment workflow automation (due diligence, monitoring, reporting) is exactly what they need
Co-Founder/CTO Bridges AI: founding engineer mindset — full ownership from ideation through post-launch; you've been this person before
M Science / Jefferies quant researcher: genuine, deep investment domain credibility — not a stretch, it's your background
MS Data Science: rigorous thinking about data quality, modeling, and statistical inference that an investment firm values

items: Chunking strategies: fixed-size (simple), semantic/sentence (coherent context), hierarchical (summary + detail), proposition-level (for dense factual docs)
Retrieval: dense (embedding cosine sim), sparse (BM25/TF-IDF), hybrid (RRF fusion) — know when hybrid beats dense-only
Reranking: cross-encoder models (ms-marco-MiniLM), LLM-as-judge reranking, Cohere Rerank / Jina Reranker
Metadata filtering + query routing: filter by fund type, date range, manager before retrieval to narrow search space
Evaluation: RAGAS metrics (faithfulness, context recall, answer relevance, context precision) — run an eval before shipping
Equi-specific: RAG over PPMs, pitchbooks, SEC filings, performance reports, meeting notes — multi-format, multi-source
Structured output extraction: tool use / function calling for reliable JSON, pydantic output schemas
Parsing multi-format docs: PDF (PyMuPDF/pdfplumber), Excel (openpyxl), email → structured investment data
Multi-step investment analysis pipelines: search → retrieve → analyze → summarize → recommend
Prompt engineering for financial tasks: few-shot with domain examples, chain-of-thought for quantitative reasoning
Streaming responses in Next.js: Vercel AI SDK, ReadableStream, incremental UI updates for long analyses
Tool use patterns: web search, calculator, document retrieval, code execution for quantitative calculations
Next.js App Router: server components (async, no client JS), server actions, route handlers, streaming UI with Suspense
TypeScript: strict mode, discriminated unions, Zod for runtime validation, tRPC for type-safe API
Auth: NextAuth.js / Clerk — JWT sessions, role-based access for client-facing vs. internal tools
Postgres: schema design for investment data (funds, managers, NAVs, returns), pgvector for embeddings, indexing strategy
Python backend: FastAPI or Flask for AI inference endpoints, Pydantic models, async with asyncio
Design a client-facing portfolio dashboard: how would you structure the schema and API?
Alternative investment data: NAV, performance (monthly/quarterly), attribution, factor exposure, drawdown series
Fund of funds data flow: manager → sub-fund → aggregate portfolio; handling different reporting formats/cadences
Data normalization: mapping heterogeneous manager reports to a canonical schema
GCP pipeline: Cloud Storage (raw docs) → Cloud Run (parsing/ETL jobs) → BigQuery (analytics) → Postgres (ops data)
dbt on BigQuery for transformation layer; Postgres as the operational database for the web app
Fund of funds structure: LP/GP, management fee (1-2%), carry (10-20%), hurdle rate, waterfall
Alternative investment types: hedge funds (L/S equity, macro, credit), PE (buyout, growth, venture), real assets, co-investments
Manager selection criteria: Sharpe ratio, max drawdown, Sortino, Calmar, factor exposure analysis, style drift detection
White-label fund products: separate accounts, sub-advised vehicles, how reporting changes at scale
AUM growth implications: more reporting, more due diligence workflows, more client portals → where AI helps most

30/60/90-day plan: Day 1-30 — understand the business deeply, map existing data flows, ship one small high-value AI tool. Day 31-60 — RAG over fund docs live. Day 61-90 — automate one due diligence workflow.
Prioritization framework: effort × impact matrix; what's reversible (build it fast) vs. irreversible (be careful)
Technical decision-making under ambiguity: spike → validate assumption → decide; avoid analysis paralysis
Team building vision: hire generalists early, keep the team small; every engineer ships to prod
Why keep the team small and flat: speed, ownership, direct feedback loop — matches their stated vision

story_beats: Why investing (must be authentic): 'I spent years as a quant equity researcher at M Science / Jefferies, working directly with alt-data to build signals. Investing isn't adjacent to my interests — it's the domain I came from. I left to build AI products, and Equi is where those two worlds converge.'

Bridges AI CTO: 'I've been the founding engineer before — I set the technical direction, built the infra, shipped features, and hired the first engineers. I know what it means to own everything and build without a safety net.'

RAG + AI systems: 'At Bridges AI I built RAG pipelines and LLM-powered workflows in production. I can take a fund document corpus and have a working retrieval system running within a day — I've done it before.'
Full-stack: 'I work across the stack. I don't hand off the frontend to someone else — I design, build, and ship the full product from database schema to UI.'
likely_questions: Why investing? What draws you to this domain specifically — be specific.
Walk me through an AI system you built end-to-end in production.
How would you approach building a RAG system over our fund documents from scratch?
What would you build in your first 30 days here?
How do you think about product decisions as an engineer? Give me an example.
Tell me about a time you had to make a significant technical decision with limited information.
How do you think about hiring and team-building as we scale?
Walk me through something you built in TypeScript / Next.js.
How would you automate the due diligence workflow for manager selection — what would the AI pipeline look like?
What's the hardest technical problem you've solved?

rounds.title: Recruiter / Hiring Screen
rounds.format: Founding-engineer continuity (Bridges AI CTO). Why investing genuinely interests you. 90s solo full-stack walkthrough (Houston Mobile Mixology).

rounds.title: Live Coding / Pairing
rounds.format: Build a small Next.js + FastAPI feature live, narrating tradeoffs - or a take-home. Server vs Client Components, Zod at boundaries, Pydantic, async.

rounds.title: System Design - RAG over investment data
rounds.format: NL Q&A over fund letters / DD memos with accurate, cited, traceable answers. Highest-leverage thing to prep deeply.

rounds.title: System Design - Full-stack / multi-tenant white-label
rounds.format: Design the white-label fund-of-funds product: multi-tenancy (RLS vs tenant_id), RBAC, partner APIs, GCP (Cloud Run / Cloud SQL / Cloud Storage).

rounds.title: Behavioral & Founder Round
rounds.format: Product-Minded / High Autonomy / Bias Toward Action. Do they want to build with you for years; do you genuinely love investing.

questions_to_ask.q: How is the engineering function structured today - am I the first technical hire, or joining a very small existing team?
What does the current investment data pipeline look like today - mostly manual analyst work the AI tooling would augment first, or existing infrastructure I'd be extending?
For the white-label product - how much customization do individual MFO/RIA partners need versus a single shared platform experience?
With the new LOI and more expected, how do you think about the engineering org's shape over the next 12-18 months - when does 'small and flat' start needing structure?

behavioral_bank.prompt: Shipped something with incomplete information. (Bias Toward Action)
behavioral_bank.story: A fast architecture decision at Bridges AI or your home-lab LiteLLM routing - calculated, reversible, iterated quickly.

behavioral_bank.prompt: Challenged a product or technical assumption. (Product-Minded)
behavioral_bank.story: A Bridges AI client instance (Valorem or Houston Mobile Mixology) where you pushed back on scope/approach with data.

behavioral_bank.prompt: Owned something end-to-end with no one to defer to. (High Autonomy)
behavioral_bank.story: Bridges AI CTO broadly, or the Houston Mobile Mixology two-part build for a real client, solo.
M Science quant equity research -> Jefferies -> alt data moving from research curiosity to decision input; now build the engineering infrastructure at a platform level.

behavioral_bank.prompt: Where do you see your career in 2-3 years?
behavioral_bank.story: Match their arc: hands-on building now, growing into leading a small flat team as the platform scales, while staying technical.

final_checklist: Rebuild the RAG architecture explanation out loud, twice, without notes
Walk through the practice full-stack build end to end
Rehearse the 5 behavioral stories mapped to Product-Minded / Autonomy / Bias Toward Action
Know fund-of-funds, MFO, LOI, manager selection cold
Have a genuine, specific answer for why investing - not 'fintech is interesting'

domain_terms.def: A fund that invests in other funds (managers) rather than directly in securities - Equi's core white-label product.
A firm managing wealth for several HNW families, often needing alt-investment access they can't source alone - Equi's client base.
Non-binding agreement signaling a deal is moving forward - Equi's growth narrative is LOI-driven right now.
Evaluating which fund managers to allocate capital to - the human expertise the AI tooling augments.

practice.title: Fund-research RAG app (practice build)
practice.desc: FastAPI + Next.js + citation-grounded RAG over synthetic fund docs. Rebuild from memory while narrating, then compare to the reference.

practice.title: 5 timed extension exercises
practice.desc: Hybrid retrieval, numeric-verification guardrail, /summary endpoint, multi-tenancy, eval set - 30-45 min each, no reference solution.

interviews.about: Woodline Partners is a multi-strategy / multi-manager hedge fund. The Sector Data Analyst role pairs fundamental/sector modeling with data analysis — building the datasets and KPIs that support a portfolio manager's stock-picking in a coverage sector.
your_strengths: M Science / Jefferies quant equity research — sector and fundamental modeling is directly your background
Strong Python + SQL for sourcing, cleaning, and modeling company/sector KPI data
MS Data Science — statistical rigor for signal validation and anomaly detection
Clear communication of analysis to non-technical stakeholders (PMs)
likely_questions: Walk me through how you'd build a fundamental model for a sector you cover.
How do you source and validate a new alternative dataset for a thesis?
Tell me about a data-driven insight that changed an investment view.
How do you decide which KPIs actually drive a stock vs. noise?
How would you communicate a complex analysis to a portfolio manager in 5 minutes?

interviews.about: The D.E. Shaw group is a global investment and technology firm. The Applied AI Engineer builds production AI systems with ML-engineering rigor — the bar is strong software engineering plus genuine ML/LLM depth, applied to real research and infrastructure problems.
your_strengths: Production RAG and LLM workflow systems built end-to-end at Bridges AI
ML-engineering discipline — testing, packaging, serving, evaluation, observability
From-scratch ML/transformer understanding (the AI Engineering track)
Quant-research background gives domain fluency for a finance-AI firm
likely_questions: Design a production RAG / LLM system end-to-end — retrieval, serving, eval.
How do you evaluate and monitor an LLM system in production?
Implement and explain a core ML component from scratch (e.g. attention).
Walk me through a hard engineering bug you debugged in an ML pipeline.
How do you reason about latency, cost, and reliability for model serving?

interviews.about: Sources, evaluates, and onboards alternative datasets for systematic and discretionary strategies — turning messy third-party data into validated, point-in-time signals while reasoning carefully about biases and licensing.
your_strengths: M Science alt-data experience — sourcing, entity resolution, signal construction
Statistical rigor: look-ahead/survivorship awareness, sampling bias, imputation
Strong data engineering — pipelines, quality gates, point-in-time correctness
Factor/IC research intuition from the Quant track
likely_questions: How would you evaluate whether a new alt-dataset has alpha?
Walk me through cleaning and entity-resolving a noisy vendor feed at scale.
How do you guard against look-ahead and survivorship bias in point-in-time data?
What are the licensing / derived-data pitfalls of re-selling signals?
How do you measure a signal's information coefficient and decay?

ai-eng.name: AI Engineering
ai-eng.summary: From-scratch implementations: math → autograd → transformers → LLMs → RAG/agents → CUDA → alignment → distributed inference.

months.title: Math Foundations & Python Mastery
months.focus: Linear algebra, probability, optimization, information theory. NumPy from scratch. Autograd engine. Cap CNN history to 10 hrs.

months.title: Classical ML — Theory to Production
months.focus: Every classical algorithm from scratch. MLflow tracking. Ship something deployed by week 8.

months.title: Deep Learning — Neural Nets to CNNs
months.focus: Autograd extended, ResNet from scratch, production image API. Cap CNN architecture history at 10 hrs total.

months.title: Sequence Models & the Transformer
months.focus: LSTM→attention→full Transformer from scratch. GPT with KV cache. BPE tokenizer. Reproduce paper BLEU within 2 points.

months.title: LLMs — Pre-training to Fine-tuning
months.focus: Pre-training pipeline, LoRA/QLoRA from scratch, vLLM inference server. Chinchilla laws. 125M model trained end-to-end.

months.title: AI Engineering — RAG, Agents, Production
months.focus: Production RAG platform, coding agent, MCP multi-agent system, RAGAS evaluation, full observability stack.

months.title: Systems — GPUs, CUDA, Distributed AI
months.focus: CUDA kernels, FlashAttention tiling, FSDP distributed training, Kubernetes ML platform.

months.title: Reinforcement Learning & Alignment
months.focus: PPO/DQN from scratch, RLHF pipeline (SFT→RM→PPO), DPO comparison, GRPO reasoning training.

months.title: Data Engineering for AI & AI Products
months.focus: Kafka, Flink, Spark, dbt. Real-time feature platform feeding live models.

months.title: Frontier — Multimodal & Research
months.focus: ViT, CLIP, diffusion, Mamba. Multimodal RAG. Paper reproduction. Research preprint drafted.

months.title: Leadership, Product & AI Startup
months.focus: AI product with real users. Open-source PR merged. Blog posts. Conference talk proposal.

months.title: Elite Synthesis — Launch & Specialization
months.focus: Distributed inference system 1000 QPS. Personal AI OS. Research preprint submitted.

ml-eng.name: ML Engineering
ml-eng.summary: The production ML lifecycle: clean code → pipelines → serving → CI/CD → monitoring → orchestration → platform. What turns a model into a system.

months.title: Engineering Foundations
months.focus: Pytest, type hints, packaging, pre-commit, clean repo structure. Treat every project as production code.

months.title: Containers & Reproducibility
months.focus: Docker, multi-stage builds, devcontainers, dependency pinning, reproducible environments.

months.title: Data Pipelines & Validation
months.focus: ETL, Great Expectations / Pandera data contracts, feature store basics, schema validation.

months.title: Experiment Tracking & Registry
months.focus: MLflow / W&B tracking, model registry, reproducible runs, artifact versioning.

months.title: Model Serving
months.focus: FastAPI inference service, batch vs online, ONNX/TorchServe, load testing, latency budgets.

months.title: CI/CD for ML
months.focus: GitHub Actions pipelines, automated tests for models, canary/blue-green deploys, rollback.

months.title: Monitoring & Drift
months.focus: Prometheus/Grafana, data & concept drift detection, alerting, SLO dashboards.

months.title: Orchestration & Retraining
months.focus: Airflow/Dagster/Prefect DAGs, scheduled retraining, backfills, idempotent jobs.

months.title: Scalable & Cheap Inference
months.focus: vLLM/TGI, autoscaling, GPU scheduling, quantization, caching, cost-per-request.

months.title: Internal ML Platform
months.focus: Self-serve tooling, templates, golden paths, abstractions other engineers reuse.

months.title: Reliability & On-call
months.focus: SLOs/SLIs, incident response, runbooks, chaos testing an ML service.

months.title: ML Systems Capstone
months.focus: End-to-end production ML system with monitoring, CI/CD, autoscaling, documented SLOs.

data-sci.name: Data Science
data-sci.summary: Statistical rigor and inference: hypothesis testing → experiments → causal inference → modeling → Bayesian → communication. The analyst muscle for the D.E. Shaw / Woodline data roles.

months.title: Statistical Inference
months.focus: Distributions, estimation, CLT, hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, bootstrap — from first principles.

months.title: Experiment Design & A/B Testing
months.focus: Power analysis, sample sizing, multiple testing, sequential tests, common A/B pitfalls.

months.title: Causal Inference
months.focus: DAGs, confounding, diff-in-diff, instrumental variables, propensity scores, regression discontinuity.

months.title: Regression & GLMs
months.focus: Linear/logistic/Poisson regression, diagnostics, regularization, interpretation, assumptions.

months.title: Time Series Forecasting
months.focus: ARIMA, state-space, exponential smoothing, backtesting forecasts, evaluation (MASE).

months.title: Feature Engineering & Leakage
months.focus: Encoding, scaling, target leakage avoidance, feature selection, robust cross-validation.

months.title: Predictive Modeling & Evaluation
months.focus: Tree ensembles, calibration, metric selection, threshold tuning, error analysis.

months.title: Bayesian Methods
months.focus: PyMC, priors, hierarchical models, MCMC diagnostics, posterior predictive checks.

months.title: Data Viz & Storytelling
months.focus: Effective charts, dashboards, narrative structure, avoiding misleading viz.

months.title: Text & Embedding Analytics
months.focus: Embeddings for analytics, topic modeling, sentiment, applying NLP to tabular+text problems.

months.title: Decision Science & Communication
months.focus: Stakeholder decks, decision frameworks, translating analysis into business action.

months.title: End-to-End DS Capstone
months.focus: Full analysis from question → data → model → recommendation, with quantified business impact.

quant.name: Quant / Alt-Data
quant.summary: The differentiator for D.E. Shaw Alt-Data and Woodline: alt-data sourcing → factor research → backtesting → portfolio/risk → research platform. Builds directly on the quant-research background.

months.title: Markets & Returns Foundations
months.focus: Returns, market microstructure, data sources, factor zoo overview, look-ahead/survivorship awareness.

months.title: Alt-Data Sourcing
months.focus: Web/satellite/transaction data, scraping pipelines, entity resolution, data cleaning at scale.

months.title: Factor Research
months.focus: Signal construction, information coefficient (IC), decay, neutralization, cross-sectional ranking.

months.title: Backtesting Framework
months.focus: Build a backtester from scratch. Handle look-ahead, survivorship, point-in-time data, realistic fills.

months.title: Portfolio Construction
months.focus: Mean-variance, risk models, constraints, optimization, factor exposure targeting.

months.title: Sector & Fundamental Modeling
months.focus: Model company/sector KPIs, fundamental factors, earnings dynamics — the Woodline core skill.

months.title: Cross-Sectional & TS Signals
months.focus: Combine multiple signals, time-series vs cross-sectional, signal blending, regime awareness.

months.title: Statistical Arbitrage
months.focus: Pairs trading, cointegration, mean reversion, hedging, capacity considerations.

months.title: Risk Management
months.focus: VaR, drawdown control, position sizing, factor risk decomposition, stress testing.

months.title: Transaction Costs & Execution
months.focus: Slippage, market impact models, turnover control, net-of-cost signal evaluation.

months.title: Quant Research Platform
months.focus: Reproducible research pipeline: data → signal → backtest → report, versioned and automated.

months.title: Alt-Data Signal Capstone
months.focus: End-to-end: source an alt-data signal, research it, backtest net-of-cost, write the research note.

research.title: Attention Is All You Need
research.why: The transformer paper. Every LLM you'll build, fine-tune, or interview about descends from this architecture — you must be able to explain self-attention from memory.
research.summary: Replaces recurrence and convolutions with a pure attention mechanism: each token attends to every other token via scaled dot-product attention, run in parallel across multiple heads. Adds positional encodings (since attention is order-agnostic), residual connections, and layernorm. The result trains far faster than RNNs and captures long-range dependencies directly. Know the Q/K/V formulation, why you divide by √d_k, and the difference between encoder self-attention, decoder masked self-attention, and cross-attention.

research.title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (GPT-3)
research.why: Established in-context learning and the scaling-laws mindset that defines how the field thinks about model capability vs. parameters and data.
research.summary: Scales a decoder-only transformer to 175B parameters and shows that a single frozen model performs new tasks from a few examples in the prompt — no gradient updates — purely via in-context learning. Documents how performance scales smoothly with model size and how prompt format (zero- vs. one- vs. few-shot) changes results. The conceptual takeaway for interviews: prompting is a capability that emerges with scale, and 'just add examples to the context' is often a viable alternative to fine-tuning.

research.title: Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback (InstructGPT)
research.why: The RLHF recipe behind ChatGPT-style assistants. This is how raw next-token predictors become aligned, useful products.
research.summary: Three stages: (1) supervised fine-tuning on human-written demonstrations, (2) train a reward model on human rankings of model outputs, (3) optimize the policy against that reward model with PPO. A 1.3B InstructGPT was preferred by humans over the 175B base GPT-3, showing alignment matters more than raw scale for usefulness. Understand the SFT → reward model → PPO pipeline and the failure modes (reward hacking, alignment tax) — DPO (a simpler, RL-free alternative) is the natural follow-up read.

research.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
research.why: The single most practical prompting technique — and the basis for agentic reasoning loops you'll design at Equi/Aaru.
research.summary: Simply prompting the model to 'think step by step' and produce intermediate reasoning before the final answer dramatically improves performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning — but only for sufficiently large models. Reasoning emerges as a scale-dependent capability. In practice this underpins self-consistency (sample multiple chains, take the majority) and ReAct-style agents. Cheap to apply, big wins on multi-step tasks; the cost is more tokens and latency.

research.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
research.why: How fine-tuning is actually done in production on a budget. You'll be expected to know parameter-efficient fine-tuning cold.
research.summary: Instead of updating all weights, freeze the pretrained model and inject small trainable low-rank matrices (A·B) into each layer; only those are trained. This cuts trainable parameters by ~10,000× and storage per task to megabytes, with no added inference latency (the deltas merge into the base weights). Multiple task-specific LoRA adapters can be swapped on one base model. QLoRA extends this to 4-bit quantized base models so you can fine-tune large models on a single GPU.

research.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
research.why: The paper that named RAG — directly the Equi system-design round (Q&A over fund documents) and the crash-course /ask endpoint.
research.summary: Couples a parametric model (a seq2seq generator) with a non-parametric memory (a dense vector index of documents). At inference it retrieves the top-k relevant passages and conditions generation on them, so knowledge lives in an updatable index rather than the weights. This reduces hallucination, lets you cite sources, and means you update knowledge by re-indexing instead of retraining. Modern RAG elaborates every stage (chunking, hybrid retrieval, reranking, eval) but the core retrieve-then-generate loop is here.

research.title: Dense Passage Retrieval for Open-Domain Question Answering
research.why: The retrieval half of RAG. Explains why embedding search beats keyword search — and, by contrast, when BM25 still wins (the case for hybrid).
research.summary: Trains a dual-encoder (separate encoders for questions and passages) with a contrastive objective so that relevant Q–passage pairs land close in embedding space, using in-batch negatives for efficient training. Dense retrieval substantially outperforms BM25 on open-domain QA because it matches on meaning, not surface tokens. The practical lesson for your RAG designs: dense retrieval handles paraphrase and synonymy but can miss exact identifiers/codes — which is exactly why production systems fuse dense + sparse (hybrid) retrieval.

research.title: Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts
research.why: A concrete, defensible reason to rerank and keep context tight — great to cite in a RAG design discussion.
research.summary: Models use information best when it sits at the very start or very end of the context window; relevant facts buried in the middle are frequently ignored, producing a U-shaped accuracy curve. Simply stuffing more retrieved chunks in does not help and can hurt. The takeaways for RAG: retrieve fewer, higher-precision chunks; rerank so the strongest evidence sits at the edges; and don't assume a long context window removes the need for good retrieval.

research.title: Introducing Contextual Retrieval
research.why: The exact technique the crash course's Day-6 /ask endpoint is built on — a cheap, high-impact upgrade to naive chunking.
research.summary: Standard chunking strips each chunk of the document context it needs to be retrievable (a chunk saying 'revenue grew 3%' loses which company and quarter). Contextual Retrieval prepends a short, LLM-generated context blurb to each chunk before embedding/indexing, and combines contextual embeddings with contextual BM25. Anthropic reports large drops in retrieval-failure rate, further improved by adding a reranking step. Prompt-caching makes generating the per-chunk context cheap. This is the pragmatic, ship-it version of everything in the academic RAG papers.

research.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
research.why: The mental model for the agentic/tool-use workflows Equi wants (due-diligence automation) and the crash-course agent work.
research.summary: Interleaves reasoning traces ('thoughts') with actions (tool/API calls) and observations in a loop: the model reasons about what to do, acts, reads the result, and reasons again. Grounding reasoning in real tool outputs reduces hallucination versus chain-of-thought alone, and the explicit trace makes the agent's behavior inspectable. This is the conceptual backbone of modern tool-using agents — function calling, retrieval, calculators, code execution all slot into the thought→action→observation cycle.

research.title: RAGAS: Automated Evaluation of Retrieval Augmented Generation
research.why: You can't ship RAG without measuring it. Maps directly to the crash-course Day-13 eval work and any 'how do you know it's good?' interview question.
research.summary: Proposes reference-free metrics to evaluate a RAG pipeline without hand-labeled answers: faithfulness (is the answer grounded in the retrieved context?), answer relevance (does it address the question?), and context precision/recall (did retrieval surface the right passages?). Metrics are computed with an LLM-as-judge. The practical discipline: separate retrieval quality from generation quality so you know which half to fix, and calibrate the LLM judge against a small human-labeled set before trusting its numbers.

research.title: A Theory for Record Linkage
research.why: The probabilistic foundation of entity resolution — the core Aaru skill and the crash-course Day-3 build. Splink is a direct modern implementation.
research.summary: Formalizes record linkage as a decision problem: for each candidate pair of records, compute a likelihood ratio from the agreement/disagreement of their fields, weighting each field by how discriminating it is (matching on a rare surname says more than matching on a common state). Compare the score to two thresholds to declare match / non-match / needs-human-review, with the thresholds set to bound false-match and false-non-match rates. This 'compare fields, weight by informativeness, threshold the score' framework is exactly what probabilistic matchers like Splink implement today — pair it with blocking (to avoid all-pairs comparison) and you have scalable entity resolution.

research.title: ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
research.why: The paper that kicked off the deep-learning era and the standard 'where did this start' reference.
research.summary: AlexNet trained a deep CNN on ImageNet and crushed the prior state of the art, halving the top-5 error of classical computer-vision pipelines. It popularized ReLU activations, dropout, data augmentation, and GPU training at scale. The result convinced the field that learned hierarchical features beat hand-engineered ones. Nearly every modern vision architecture descends from its design choices.

research.title: Distributed Representations of Words and Phrases and their Compositionality
research.why: Introduced the dense word-embedding intuition that underpins all modern vector search and retrieval.
research.summary: word2vec learns low-dimensional word vectors by predicting context words, using the efficient skip-gram model with negative sampling. The embeddings capture semantic and syntactic structure so well that analogies become vector arithmetic (king − man + woman ≈ queen). It made dense distributed representations practical at web scale. This is the conceptual ancestor of today's text embeddings and similarity search.

research.title: Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes (VAE)
research.why: Foundational generative model that introduced the reparameterization trick used throughout probabilistic deep learning.
research.summary: The VAE frames generative modeling as learning a latent-variable model trained by maximizing a variational lower bound (the ELBO). Its key contribution, the reparameterization trick, lets you backpropagate through random sampling. This yields a smooth, continuous latent space you can sample from and interpolate over. VAEs remain a core building block and appear inside modern systems like latent diffusion.

research.title: Very Deep Convolutional Networks for Large-Scale Image Recognition (VGG)
research.why: Established the depth-with-small-filters pattern and remains a common backbone / feature extractor.
research.summary: VGG showed that stacking many small 3×3 convolutions into very deep, uniform networks substantially improves accuracy. Its simple, repetitive architecture made it easy to reason about and reuse. Pretrained VGG features became a default choice for transfer learning and perceptual losses. The design clarified that depth, not exotic filters, was a primary driver of CNN performance.

research.title: Going Deeper with Convolutions (GoogLeNet / Inception)
research.why: Introduced the efficiency-minded Inception module and 1×1 bottleneck convolutions still used to control compute.
research.summary: GoogLeNet introduced the Inception module, which runs convolutions of multiple kernel sizes in parallel and concatenates them, capturing multi-scale features. It used 1×1 convolutions as cheap dimensionality-reduction bottlenecks to keep parameter count low. The result was a deep, accurate network far more compute-efficient than its peers. This efficiency-vs-accuracy framing recurs throughout production model design.

research.title: Sequence to Sequence Learning with Neural Networks
research.why: The encoder–decoder framework every modern generative / translation model conceptually builds on.
research.summary: This paper showed an LSTM encoder could compress an input sequence into a fixed vector that a second LSTM decoder expands into an output sequence, enabling end-to-end machine translation. It established the encoder–decoder paradigm for variable-length input/output. A notable trick was reversing the source sentence to improve optimization. It set the stage for attention and ultimately transformers.

research.title: Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate
research.why: The original attention mechanism — understanding it is the foundation for understanding transformers.
research.summary: This work fixed the bottleneck of compressing an entire sentence into one vector by letting the decoder attend to a weighted combination of all encoder states at each step. This soft alignment dramatically improved translation of long sentences. It introduced the core attention idea later generalized into self-attention. It is the direct intellectual precursor to 'Attention Is All You Need.'

research.title: Generative Adversarial Nets (GAN)
research.why: Defined adversarial training — a foundational generative paradigm and a frequent conceptual interview topic.
research.summary: GANs pit a generator that fabricates samples against a discriminator that tries to tell real from fake, training both in a minimax game. As the discriminator improves, the generator is pushed to produce increasingly realistic outputs. This adversarial framework produced a wave of high-fidelity image-synthesis research. It introduced training dynamics and instabilities that motivated much follow-on work.

research.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization
research.why: The default optimizer you configure in virtually every training job — expect questions on how it works.
research.summary: Adam combines momentum (a running average of gradients) with per-parameter adaptive learning rates (a running average of squared gradients), plus bias correction for the early steps. This makes it robust to sparse gradients and largely insensitive to hyperparameter tuning. It became the de facto optimizer for deep learning. Understanding its first/second-moment estimates is standard ML-engineering knowledge.

research.title: Dropout: A Simple Way to Prevent Neural Networks from Overfitting
research.why: Core regularization technique and a classic 'how do you prevent overfitting' interview answer.
research.summary: Dropout randomly zeroes a fraction of neurons during each training step, preventing co-adaptation and forcing redundant, robust representations. At test time all units are used with scaled weights, approximating an ensemble average over many sub-networks. It provided a cheap, effective regularizer that became standard in deep nets. The ensemble interpretation is a frequently asked conceptual point.

research.title: Batch Normalization
research.why: Made very deep networks trainable and is a staple normalization question in ML interviews.
research.summary: Batch normalization standardizes layer activations using per-minibatch mean and variance, then rescales with learned parameters. This stabilizes and speeds up training, allows higher learning rates, and acts as a mild regularizer. It became near-universal in CNNs and reshaped how people think about optimization landscapes. The train-vs-inference behavior (running statistics) is a common gotcha worth knowing.

research.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition (ResNet)
research.why: Residual / skip connections are everywhere (including transformers); one of the most-cited, most-asked papers.
research.summary: ResNet introduced skip connections that let a layer learn a residual function relative to its input, so identity mappings are easy to preserve. This solved the degradation problem where adding layers hurt accuracy, enabling networks hundreds of layers deep. Residual connections turned out to be essential for gradient flow and are now a default building block, including inside transformer blocks. It is arguably the single most influential architecture paper of the decade.

research.title: U-Net: Convolutional Networks for Biomedical Image Segmentation
research.why: The encoder–decoder-with-skips backbone reused as the denoising network inside modern diffusion models.
research.summary: U-Net pairs a contracting encoder with an expanding decoder and adds skip connections that pass high-resolution features across, enabling precise dense pixel-level predictions from little data. Originally for biomedical segmentation, its symmetric design generalized far beyond it. Today it is the standard noise-prediction backbone in diffusion image generators. Knowing U-Net is essential context for how Stable Diffusion works under the hood.

research.title: Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network
research.why: The basis for shrinking large models into cheap deployable ones — a core production-AI concern.
research.summary: Knowledge distillation trains a small student model to mimic the soft probability outputs of a large teacher, using a temperature to expose the teacher's 'dark knowledge' about class similarities. The student often matches teacher accuracy at a fraction of the size and cost. It is a foundational technique for model compression and efficient serving. Modern distilled LLMs and small on-device models trace directly to this idea.

research.title: Unsupervised Representation Learning with Deep Convolutional GANs (DCGAN)
research.why: Stabilized GAN training and showed latent spaces support semantic interpolation — a key generative-AI concept.
research.summary: DCGAN provided architectural guidelines (strided convolutions, batch norm, no fully connected layers) that made GAN training reliably stable for images. It demonstrated smooth latent-space arithmetic and interpolation over generated faces and scenes. This bridged GANs from a fragile idea to a practical image-generation tool. It also showed GANs learn useful unsupervised representations.

research.title: Layer Normalization
research.why: The normalization actually used inside transformers / LLMs — more relevant to NLP than batchnorm.
research.summary: Layer normalization normalizes across the features of a single example rather than across the batch, making it independent of batch size and well-suited to sequence models. This batch-independence is exactly why transformers adopt it instead of batch norm. It stabilizes training of attention-based architectures. Variants like RMSNorm and the pre-norm vs post-norm placement debate all build on it.

research.title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers
research.why: Defined the pretrain-then-finetune encoder paradigm and still powers many embedding / retrieval systems.
research.summary: BERT pretrains a bidirectional transformer encoder using masked language modeling (predict hidden tokens from both directions) plus next-sentence prediction. The resulting representations transfer to a wide range of NLP tasks with light fine-tuning, setting new state of the art across the board. It established the now-standard pretrain/fine-tune workflow. Encoder models in this lineage remain the backbone of many text-embedding and reranking systems.

research.title: DistilBERT: smaller, faster, cheaper, lighter
research.why: A concrete, widely deployed case study of distilling a transformer for cheaper production inference.
research.summary: DistilBERT applies knowledge distillation during pretraining to compress BERT by ~40% while keeping ~97% of its performance and running ~60% faster. It uses a triple loss combining language modeling, distillation, and cosine-embedding alignment with the teacher. It became a go-to lightweight model for latency- and cost-sensitive deployments. It is a practical demonstration of distillation applied to transformers.

research.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
research.why: Quantified how loss scales with model / data / compute — the reasoning that justifies modern LLM scaling.
research.summary: This paper showed that language-model loss falls as a smooth power law in model size, dataset size, and compute, with remarkably predictable behavior across orders of magnitude. It argued larger models are more sample-efficient and gave guidance on allocating a fixed compute budget. These empirical laws underpinned the strategic decision to scale up aggressively. It is essential context for any conversation about why LLMs got big (later refined by Chinchilla).

research.title: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM)
research.why: The paper that made diffusion models competitive and launched the modern image-generation wave.
research.summary: DDPM defines a forward process that gradually adds Gaussian noise to data and trains a network to reverse it step by step, recovering samples from pure noise. It reframed the training objective as a simple noise-prediction loss, making diffusion practical and high quality. This dethroned GANs as the leading image generators. It is the technical foundation beneath Stable Diffusion, DALL·E 2, and friends.

research.title: A Simple Framework for Contrastive Learning of Visual Representations (SimCLR)
research.why: Canonical self-supervised / contrastive learning method; the loss intuition recurs across embeddings and retrieval.
research.summary: SimCLR learns image representations without labels by pulling together two augmented views of the same image and pushing apart views of different images, using the InfoNCE contrastive loss. It showed that strong data augmentation, a projection head, and large batches are the keys to making this work. The learned features rival supervised pretraining when fine-tuned. Its contrastive recipe is the template for much modern representation and embedding learning.

research.title: An Image is Worth 16×16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition (ViT)
research.why: Unified vision and language under one transformer architecture — a key step toward multimodal models.
research.summary: The Vision Transformer splits an image into fixed-size patches, embeds them as tokens, and feeds them to a standard transformer encoder with no convolutions. Given enough pretraining data, it matches or beats top CNNs, showing convolutional inductive biases are not strictly necessary. This convergence on a single architecture across modalities enabled today's multimodal systems. ViT backbones now power CLIP, modern detectors, and vision-language models.

research.title: Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision (CLIP)
research.why: The shared image-text embedding space behind multimodal search, zero-shot classification, and diffusion conditioning.
research.summary: CLIP trains an image encoder and a text encoder jointly with a contrastive objective over 400M image-caption pairs, aligning both into a shared embedding space. This enables zero-shot classification by comparing an image to text prompts, with no task-specific fine-tuning. The embeddings became a workhorse for multimodal retrieval and for conditioning generative models. It is a cornerstone of applied multimodal AI.

research.title: RoFormer: Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE)
research.why: RoPE is the positional encoding used in LLaMA and most modern LLMs, and underlies context-length extension.
research.summary: RoPE encodes token position by rotating query and key vectors by an angle proportional to their position, so attention naturally depends on relative distance. This gives clean relative-position behavior and extrapolates better to longer sequences than learned absolute embeddings. It became the standard positional scheme in contemporary open LLMs. Understanding RoPE is central to discussions of context-window extension.

research.title: High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models
research.why: The architecture behind Stable Diffusion — the most production-relevant generative-image paper to know.
research.summary: Latent diffusion runs the diffusion process in a compressed latent space produced by a pretrained autoencoder rather than directly on pixels, slashing compute while preserving quality. Cross-attention layers inject conditioning such as text prompts. This made high-resolution text-to-image generation feasible on consumer GPUs and became Stable Diffusion. It is the canonical reference for how modern image generators are engineered.

research.title: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models (Chinchilla)
research.why: Reset how the field balances model size vs training tokens; 'Chinchilla-optimal' is standard interview knowledge.
research.summary: Chinchilla revisited scaling laws and found prior LLMs were badly undertrained: for a fixed compute budget, model size and training tokens should scale roughly equally. A 70B model trained on far more data beat the much larger 280B Gopher. This shifted the field toward smaller-but-more-trained models. The token-to-parameter ratio it popularized still guides pretraining decisions today.

research.title: FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention
research.why: The GPU-level optimization that makes long-context transformers practical — a hot systems-engineering topic.
research.summary: FlashAttention computes exact attention without ever materializing the full N×N attention matrix in slow GPU memory, instead tiling the computation and fusing it in fast on-chip SRAM. This makes attention memory-linear and substantially faster by being IO-aware about the GPU memory hierarchy. It enabled longer context windows and cheaper training/inference. It is now a default kernel in modern LLM stacks and a favorite systems interview topic.

research.title: LLM.int8(): 8-bit Matrix Multiplication for Transformers at Scale
research.why: Made it possible to run large models on far less hardware; quantization is a core deployment skill.
research.summary: LLM.int8() quantizes most transformer matrix multiplications to 8-bit integers while isolating a small number of high-magnitude 'outlier' features in 16-bit, preserving accuracy. This roughly halves memory for inference with essentially no quality loss, letting large models fit on smaller GPUs. It shipped in the widely used bitsandbytes library. It is a foundational reference for the now-essential practice of LLM quantization.

research.title: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models
research.why: The open-weights model that catalyzed the entire local / open-source LLM ecosystem engineers now build on.
research.summary: LLaMA showed that relatively small models trained on far more tokens (following Chinchilla-style reasoning) can match or beat much larger models like GPT-3. It consolidated modern architecture choices, including RMSNorm, SwiGLU activations, and rotary embeddings. Its release seeded a massive open-source fine-tuning and tooling ecosystem. It is the practical foundation behind a large share of today's deployed open LLMs.

research.title: Mixtral of Experts
research.why: The clearest modern reference for sparse Mixture-of-Experts — the efficiency technique behind many frontier models.
research.summary: Mixtral is a sparse Mixture-of-Experts model where a router selects 2 of 8 feed-forward experts per token, so only a fraction of the total parameters activate for any given input. This gives the quality of a much larger dense model at the inference cost of a small one. It matched or beat larger dense models while being far cheaper to serve. It is the go-to concrete example for explaining how sparse MoE trades total capacity against active compute.

practice._comment: Problem banks for interview prep. Each section → groups → problems. Problem keys: n=name, d=difficulty (E/M/H), s=1 marks Blind-75, u=leetcode slug (app builds https://leetcode.com/problems/{u}/), url=full link for non-leetcode items. Solved state is tracked client-side in localStorage.

sections.title: DSA — NeetCode 150
sections.note: Work pattern by pattern, not problem by problem. Do the Blind 75 (★) first for breadth, then fill in the rest of each pattern. Premium-locked problems have no direct link — solve them on NeetCode.

problems.n: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
Remove Nth Node From End of List
Lowest Common Ancestor of a BST
Count Good Nodes in Binary Tree
Kth Smallest Element in a BST
Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
Kth Largest Element in a Stream
Kth Largest Element in an Array
Letter Combinations of a Phone Number
Number of Connected Components in an Undirected Graph
Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock With Cooldown
Merge Triplets to Form Target Triplet
Minimum Interval to Include Each Query

sections.title: SQL
sections.note: The Top SQL 50 covers the patterns that come up in data/analyst/ML interviews: joins, aggregation, ranking, and window functions. Get the window-function block automatic.

problems.n: Employees Earning More Than Their Managers
Group Sold Products By The Date

sections.title: Data Science & ML
sections.note: Pandas problems are on LeetCode (Introduction to Pandas). The stats/probability and ML items are conceptual — say the answer out loud or whiteboard the derivation, then tick it.

problems.n: Explain p-value, Type I/II error, and statistical power
Compute A/B test sample size for a target MDE
Maximum likelihood vs MAP — derive for a Gaussian
State and apply the Central Limit Theorem
Bootstrap a confidence interval from scratch
Bayes' theorem: the disease-testing / base-rate problem
Confounders, colliders, and DAGs — when does correlation imply causation?
L1 vs L2 regularization — effect on weights and when to use each
ROC/AUC vs PR curve — which to use when
Handle class imbalance — resampling, weighting, threshold tuning
Gradient boosting vs random forest — bias/variance trade-off
Interpret a model with feature importance / SHAP
Evaluate an LLM/RAG system — offline, online, and human eval

sections.title: System Design & Cases
sections.note: For each prompt, talk through requirements → high-level design → data model → scaling/bottlenecks → trade-offs. The first two map directly to your Aaru and Equi rounds.

problems.n: RAG over fund documents with cited, traceable answers (Equi)
Ingest a 100TB alt-data source + entity resolution (Aaru)
Real-time feature store + low-latency model serving
Define success metrics for a new feature
Should we launch feature X? (experiment design + decision)
Prioritize a roadmap under limited engineering capacity

sections.title: Quant (for fun)
sections.note: Probability brainteasers and market intuition — the kind D.E. Shaw / Woodline-adjacent quant chats throw out. Optional, but genuinely fun and sharpens the stats muscle.

problems.n: Expected flips to get HH vs HT
Gambler's ruin — probability of reaching N before 0
Expected value of the max of two dice
Coupon collector — expected draws for all N
Random walk — probability of return to the origin
Monty Hall and its N-door generalization
Why is a call worth more when volatility is higher?
How would you quote a two-sided market as a market maker?
Kelly criterion — derive the optimal bet fraction
Expected PnL of a biased coin-flip betting game

sections.title: Behavioral (100 Qs)
sections.note: The top ~100 behavioral and motivation questions across the themes these interviews filter for. Map each to a concrete STAR story from your bank (M Science, Bridges AI, CU Boulder), rehearse it to ~90s, then tick it. Reuse stories across themes.

problems.n: Walk me through your resume / career arc.
Why now — why are you leaving / making this move?
Why are you moving from founder/CTO back to an IC engineering role?
What are you looking for in your next role?
Where do you see yourself in 2–3 years?
What's your greatest strength? Greatest weakness?
What questions do you have for us?
Tell me about a time you led a project end-to-end.
Describe a time you influenced a decision without authority.
Tell me about a time you mentored or unblocked a teammate.
Describe a time you set technical direction for a team.
Tell me about a time you drove alignment among stakeholders.
How have you hired or built a team?
Describe a time you had to motivate others through a hard stretch.
Tell me about a time you took initiative beyond your role.
Tell me about a disagreement with a coworker — how did it resolve?
Describe disagreeing with your manager / a senior person.
Tell me about a time you were wrong in a debate and changed your mind.
How do you handle a teammate who isn't pulling their weight?
Describe defending an unpopular technical position.
Tell me about a conflict over priorities between teams.
How do you disagree and commit?
Describe giving difficult feedback to a peer.
Tell me about your biggest professional failure.
Describe a project that didn't go as planned.
Tell me about a bug/outage you caused — and what you learned.
Describe a decision you'd make differently in hindsight.
Tell me about a time you missed a deadline.
How do you handle criticism of your work?
Describe a time you took a risk that didn't pay off.
Tell me about feedback that was hard to hear.
Tell me about something you owned end-to-end with no one to defer to.
Describe going above and beyond what was asked.
Tell me about a time you fixed something nobody asked you to.
How do you operate with little direction or process?
Describe taking responsibility for a team failure.
Tell me about a long-term bet you championed.
How do you decide what to work on when nobody tells you?
Tell me about a decision made with incomplete information.
Describe a time requirements were unclear — how did you proceed?
How do you make a reversible vs irreversible decision?
Tell me about a high-stakes call you made under pressure.
Describe choosing between two strong technical options.
How do you avoid analysis paralysis?
Tell me about a time you had to change course mid-project.
Describe a time you said no to a request.
Tell me about a great team you were part of — why did it work?
Describe working with a difficult stakeholder.
Tell me about collaborating across functions (design/PM/data).
How do you onboard onto an unfamiliar codebase or team?
Describe a time you helped the team over your own task.
Tell me about building consensus on an approach.
How do you work with people more/less experienced than you?
How do you prioritize when everything is urgent?
Tell me about juggling multiple projects at once.
Describe cutting scope to hit a deadline.
Tell me about a time you shipped under significant time pressure.
How do you estimate and plan a project?
Tell me about a time you had to push back on scope.
How do you handle being overcommitted?
Tell me about shipping something fast with incomplete information.
Describe challenging a product or technical assumption.
Tell me about a time you simplified an over-engineered solution.
How do you balance speed vs quality / tech debt?
Describe an idea you pushed from concept to launch.
Tell me about using data to drive a product decision.
How do you think about the user/customer as an engineer?
Explain a complex technical concept to a non-technical audience.
Tell me about presenting work to leadership.
Describe writing a doc/spec that drove a decision.
How do you keep up with a fast-moving field?
Tell me about teaching yourself something hard, fast.
Describe a piece of feedback that changed how you work.
What's something you've learned recently that excites you?
How do you handle stress and avoid burnout?
Why do you want to be a founding engineer again?
What would you build in your first 30/60/90 days?
How do you operate without process or specs?
How do you decide what NOT to build?
Tell me about wearing many hats at once.
How do you think about technical debt at an early-stage company?
Why investing / fintech — and why is it authentic for you?
What do you find interesting about fund-of-funds / alt investments?
How does your quant-research background apply here?
What's a recent thing in markets/AI you found interesting?
How would AI most help an investment workflow today?
What would you want to learn about the business in month one?

day_of_week.Monday: Set weekly goals per track, review last week's deliverables
day_of_week.Friday: Write 500+ words technical content, review the week
day_of_week.Sunday: Review notes, plan next week, update GitHub
